![Image 2](https://www.journalofdemocracy.org/wp-content/uploads/2019/12/31.1-Levitsky-photo-1920x1080.jpg)

The New Competitive Authoritarianism
====================================

*   [Steven Levitsky](https://www.journalofdemocracy.org/authors/steven-levitsky/)
*   [Lucan A. Way](https://www.journalofdemocracy.org/authors/lucan-a-way/)

**Issue Date**[January 2020](https://www.journalofdemocracy.org/issue/january-2020/)

**Volume** 31 

**Issue** 1 

**Page Numbers** 51-65 

[Print](javascript:window.print())

[Download from Project MUSE](https://muse.jhu.edu/article/745953)

[View Citation](https://www.journalofdemocracy.org/articles/the-new-competitive-authoritarianism/#citation)

**Select your citation format:**

Copy

Read the full essay [here](https://muse.jhu.edu/article/745953).

Competitive authoritarianism—in which the coexistence of meaningful democratic institutions and serious incumbent abuse yields electoral competition that is real but unfair—is alive and well, nearly two decades after the concept was introduced in the _Journal of Democracy_. This is surprising, because the Western liberal hegemony of the 1990s, which led many full autocracies to become competitive authoritarian, has waned. Competitive politics persists because many autocrats lack the coercive and organizational capacity to consolidate hegemonic rule, and because the alternatives to multiparty elections lack legitimacy across the globe. Recently, new competitive authoritarian regimes have emerged in countries with strong democratic institutions, raising concerns about the diffusion of competitive authoritarianism to the West.

About the Authors
-----------------

### Steven Levitsky

_**Steven Levitsky**_ _is David Rockefeller Professor of Latin American Studies and professor of government and director of the David Rockefeller Center for Latin American Studies at Harvard University._

[View all work by Steven Levitsky](https://www.journalofdemocracy.org/authors/steven-levitsky/)

### Lucan A. Way

**_Lucan Way_**_is Distinguished Professor of Democracy at the University of Toronto, co-director of the Petro Jacyk Program for the Study of Ukraine, and co-chair of the_ Journal of Democracy _Editorial Board._

[View all work by Lucan A. Way](https://www.journalofdemocracy.org/authors/lucan-a-way/)

Image Credit: Sean Gallup/Getty Images

Volume 32, Issue 3

#### [Latin America Erupts: When Does Competitive Authoritarianism Take Root?](https://www.journalofdemocracy.org/articles/latin-america-erupts-when-does-competitive-authoritarianism-take-root/)

*   [V. Ximena Velasco Guachalla](https://www.journalofdemocracy.org/authors/v-ximena-velasco-guachalla/)

*   [Amy Erica Smith](https://www.journalofdemocracy.org/authors/amy-erica-smith/)

It is not easy to build a stable hybrid regime. Elected autocrats may try, but comparing Bolivia, Brazil, and Venezuela shows how difficult it is to succeed.

Volume 13, Issue 2

#### [Elections Without Democracy: The Menu of Manipulation](https://www.journalofdemocracy.org/articles/elections-without-democracy-the-menu-of-manipulation/)

*   [Andreas Schedler](https://www.journalofdemocracy.org/authors/andreas-schedler/)

Elections, usually taken to be a hallmark of democracy, can also become a tool of authoritarian powerholders seeking to legitimate their rule.

#### [Cambodia’s Transition to Hegemonic Authoritarianism](https://www.journalofdemocracy.org/articles/cambodias-transition-to-hegemonic-authoritarianism/)

*   [Lee Morgenbesser](https://www.journalofdemocracy.org/authors/lee-morgenbesser/)

A crackdown on the opposition, followed by sham parliamentary elections in July 2018, has deepened and extended the decades-long personalist dictatorship of Hun Sen.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Levitsky and Way conceptualize competitive authoritarianism, illustrating how democratic institutions coexist with significant abuses, creating electoral competition that is real yet fundamentally unfair.

Comparative studies analyze various regimes, emphasizing how competitive authoritarianism emerges in states with nominal democratic structures, impacting the understanding of governance and political dynamics.

enriched_points.point_title: Impact of external pressures on democratization
enriched_points.expanded_content: External pressures, such as international sanctions and diplomatic interventions, significantly influence democratization efforts, shaping the resilience and adaptability of competitive authoritarian regimes.
